message.content:
ROUND 2 for task **T108** (goals:G6) — edge default-route wiring. Your round-1 work is at commit `d825b23288905a8d0727f6f8f1e6b1a0af3b1ec4` (internal/device/route_linux.go, route_other.go, route_test.go, device.go; test/e2e/default_route_test.go; docs/install.md; wanbond.example.toml). In YOUR isolated worktree: `git reset --hard d825b23288905a8d0727f6f8f1e6b1a0af3b1ec4` (sanctioned), then `git switch -c t108-fix`. Report `branch: t108-fix` + resultCommit.

The review panel DISAPPROVED (1 of 2 reviewers) with TWO objective lifecycle defects in the new route code. Fix BOTH:

**Criticism 1 — route install is NOT idempotent; unrecoverable bring-up loop after unclean exit under tun_persist=true.** `programRoute` (internal/device/route_linux.go) adds with `NLM_F_CREATE|NLM_F_EXCL`. With `tun_persist=true` (shipped in T109), an unclean daemon death (SIGKILL/OOM/panic) leaves wanbond0 AND its two /1 routes in place (Close never ran). On restart, the first RTM_NEWROUTE returns EEXIST, `up()` aborts, and every subsequent restart fails identically until an operator manually runs `ip route del`. The daemon's posture elsewhere is to ADOPT leftover persistent-TUN state on restart (see internal/device/persist_linux.go / the T109 tun_persist adoption) — the route install MUST match. Fix: in the add path, treat `unix.EEXIST` as SUCCESS (adopt the existing route), OR use `NLM_F_REPLACE` instead of `NLM_F_EXCL`. Prefer the approach that leaves the route in exactly the intended state (NLM_F_REPLACE|NLM_F_CREATE cleanly overwrites; EEXIST-tolerance adopts as-is — pick REPLACE so a stale route with wrong attributes is corrected). This ALSO fixes a hard-fail on any duplicate prefix in the computed set. Add/adjust a unit test in route_test.go proving re-install of an already-present route succeeds (you can factor the EEXIST/replace decision into a pure helper if a real netlink socket isn't available in the sandbox; otherwise assert the flag choice).

**Criticism 2 — partial-install leak under tun_persist=true + factually wrong comment.** `installRoutes`' comment claims a mid-set failure leaves 'every already-installed prefix left for Close to withdraw', but on install failure `up()` returns BEFORE the `Tunnel` is constructed, so `Close`/`removeRoutes` never runs; under `tun_persist=true` the already-installed routes leak on the surviving interface (then trigger criticism 1's EEXIST on retry). Fix: on the install-error path in `up()` (internal/device/device.go), best-effort `removeRoutes(name, prefixes)` (ignore errors) BEFORE returning the error, and correct the now-wrong `installRoutes` comment.

Note: with criticism 1 fixed via NLM_F_REPLACE, criticism 2's leak becomes benign on the NEXT successful start (REPLACE overwrites), but the partial-install cleanup on the SAME failed start is still correct to do (don't leave half a default route installed when bring-up failed) — implement both.

STRICT: stay within Q41 (routes only — no policy routing/SNAT/forwarding). Do NOT change config validation (the multiple-default-route-peers gap is filed as D59, out of scope). Keep the change minimal and grounded.

Gate before commit: `nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./... && go build -tags e2e ./... && go vet -tags e2e ./...'` (privileged e2e exec DEFERRED to hosts — compile+vet only). Commit on `t108-fix`. Return the standard structured result JSON {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary}. Do NOT mutate the ledger, do NOT merge. End with a `### Session summary` block.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
internal/device/route_linux.go
internal/device/route_other.go
internal/device/route_test.go
test/e2e/default_route_test.go

1	//go:build linux
2	
3	package device
4	
5	import (
6		"encoding/binary"
7		"errors"
8		"fmt"
9		"net/netip"
10	
11		"golang.org/x/sys/unix"
12	)
13	
14	// Route programming for mode=default-route (I6, Q41). When a peer opts into
15	// full-tunnel mode, the daemon installs the wg-quick-style split default route
16	// (the two /1 prefixes) INTO wanbond0 after the interface is up, and withdraws
17	// them on Close. It talks rtnetlink directly over an AF_NETLINK socket — matching
18	// the daemon's "no privileged shell-outs" posture (device.go) and the raw
19	// golang.org/x/sys/unix idiom already used for the SIOCSIFFLAGS link-up (see
20	// linkup_linux.go) — rather than shelling out to `ip route`.
21	//
22	// STRICT Q41 boundary: this installs plain scope-link device routes ONLY. It does
23	// NOT add policy-routing rules (ip rule / fwmark / suppress_prefixlength), SNAT, or
24	// any concentrator ip_forward/MASQUERADE/FORWARD programming — those stay documented
25	// C3/C6 operator recipes. The two /1s (rather than a literal /0) deliberately do NOT
26	// override the physical default route, so the encrypted underlay to the concentrator
27	// endpoint is unaffected (the same reason wg-quick uses the /1 split).
28	
29	// rtmsgLen is sizeof(struct rtmsg): family, dst_len, src_len, tos, table,
30	// protocol, scope, type (8×u8) followed by flags (u32) — 12 bytes.
31	const rtmsgLen = 12
32	
33	// nlaAlign rounds n up to the netlink attribute alignment (NLA_ALIGNTO = 4).
34	func nlaAlign(n int) int { return (n + 3) &^ 3 }
35	
36	// ifIndex resolves an interface name to its kernel ifindex via SIOCGIFINDEX,
37	// mirroring the ioctl idiom in linkup_linux.go. A missing interface surfaces the
38	// raw errno (unix.ENODEV), which the removal path treats as "already gone".
39	func ifIndex(name string) (int, error) {
40		fd, err := unix.Socket(unix.AF_INET, unix.SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
41		if err != nil {
42			return 0, fmt.Errorf("open control socket: %w", err)
43		}
44		defer func() { _ = unix.Close(fd) }()
45	
46		ifr, err := unix.NewIfreq(name)
47		if err != nil {
48			return 0, fmt.Errorf("build ifreq for %q: %w", name, err)
49		}
50		if err := unix.IoctlIfreq(fd, unix.SIOCGIFINDEX, ifr); err != nil {
51			return 0, fmt.Errorf("SIOCGIFINDEX %q: %w", name, err)
52		}
53		return int(ifr.Uint32()), nil
54	}
55	
56	// routeSocket opens and binds an AF_NETLINK rtnetlink socket for one batch of
57	// route requests. The caller closes it.
58	func routeSocket() (int, error) {
59		fd, err := unix.Socket(unix.AF_NETLINK, unix.SOCK_RAW|unix.SOCK_CLOEXEC, unix.NETLINK_ROUTE)
60		if err != nil {
61			return -1, fmt.Errorf("open rtnetlink socket: %w", err)
62		}
63		if err := unix.Bind(fd, &unix.SockaddrNetlink{Family: unix.AF_NETLINK}); err != nil {
64			_ = unix.Close(fd)
65			return -1, fmt.Errorf("bind rtnetlink socket: %w", err)
66		}
67		return fd, nil
68	}
69	
70	// programRoute sends a single RTM_NEWROUTE (add) or RTM_DELROUTE (remove) request
71	// for the scope-link device route prefix p via ifindex, then waits for the kernel
72	// ACK. The request carries exactly two attributes — RTA_DST (the masked prefix
73	// address) and RTA_OIF (the output interface) — with no gateway, so the kernel
74	// installs an onlink device route. The returned error, on a non-zero ACK, wraps the
75	// kernel errno so the caller can classify it (e.g. ESRCH/ENOENT on a delete of an
76	// already-absent route).
77	func programRoute(fd int, seq uint32, add bool, ifindex int, p netip.Prefix) error {
78		msgType := uint16(unix.RTM_DELROUTE)
79		flags := uint16(unix.NLM_F_REQUEST | unix.NLM_F_ACK)
80		if add {
81			msgType = unix.RTM_NEWROUTE
82			flags |= unix.NLM_F_CREATE | unix.NLM_F_EXCL
83		}
84	
85		addr := p.Addr()
86		family := byte(unix.AF_INET)
87		var ab []byte
88		if addr.Is6() {
89			family = unix.AF_INET6
90			a := addr.As16()
91			ab = a[:]
92		} else {
93			a := addr.As4()
94			ab = a[:]
95		}
96	
97		en := binary.NativeEndian
98		rtaDstLen := 4 + len(ab)
99		const rtaOifLen = 8
100		total := unix.SizeofNlMsghdr + rtmsgLen + nlaAlign(rtaDstLen) + rtaOifLen
101	
102		buf := make([]byte, total)
103		// nlmsghdr
104		en.PutUint32(buf[0:4], uint32(total))
105		en.PutUint16(buf[4:6], msgType)
106		en.PutUint16(buf[6:8], flags)
107		en.PutUint32(buf[8:12], seq)
108		en.PutUint32(buf[12:16], 0) // pid 0: the kernel is the peer
109	
110		// rtmsg
111		off := unix.SizeofNlMsghdr
112		buf[off+0] = family
113		buf[off+1] = byte(p.Bits()) // dst_len
114		buf[off+2] = 0              // src_len
115		buf[off+3] = 0              // tos
116		buf[off+4] = unix.RT_TABLE_MAIN
117		buf[off+5] = unix.RTPROT_BOOT
118		buf[off+6] = unix.RT_SCOPE_LINK
119		buf[off+7] = unix.RTN_UNICAST
120		en.PutUint32(buf[off+8:off+12], 0) // rtm_flags
121	
122		// RTA_DST
123		off += rtmsgLen
124		en.PutUint16(buf[off+0:off+2], uint16(rtaDstLen))
125		en.PutUint16(buf[off+2:off+4], unix.RTA_DST)
126		copy(buf[off+4:], ab)
127	
128		// RTA_OIF
129		off += nlaAlign(rtaDstLen)
130		en.PutUint16(buf[off+0:off+2], uint16(rtaOifLen))
131		en.PutUint16(buf[off+2:off+4], unix.RTA_OIF)
132		en.PutUint32(buf[off+4:off+8], uint32(ifindex))
133	
134		sa := &unix.SockaddrNetlink{Family: unix.AF_NETLINK}
135		if err := unix.Sendto(fd, buf, 0, sa); err != nil {
136			return fmt.Errorf("send rtnetlink request: %w", err)
137		}
138		return recvAck(fd)
139	}
140	
141	// recvAck reads the kernel's NLMSG_ERROR acknowledgement for the request just sent
142	// on fd and walks the returned netlink messages. A zero error code is the success ACK
143	// (NLM_F_ACK); a non-zero code is returned as the corresponding unix.Errno. Each
144	// message is an nlmsghdr (SizeofNlMsghdr bytes: len u32, type u16, flags u16, seq u32,
145	// pid u32) followed by its payload; an NLMSG_ERROR payload begins with an int32 errno
146	// (struct nlmsgerr). Messages are NLMSG_ALIGNTO(=4)-aligned, the same alignment as
147	// nlaAlign.
148	func recvAck(fd int) error {
149		buf := make([]byte, unix.Getpagesize())
150		n, _, err := unix.Recvfrom(fd, buf, 0)
151		if err != nil {
152			return fmt.Errorf("recv rtnetlink ack: %w", err)
153		}
154		en := binary.NativeEndian
155		b := buf[:n]
156		for len(b) >= unix.SizeofNlMsghdr {
157			msgLen := int(en.Uint32(b[0:4]))
158			msgType := en.Uint16(b[4:6])
159			if msgLen < unix.SizeofNlMsghdr || msgLen > len(b) {
160				return fmt.Errorf("malformed rtnetlink ack: message length %d in %d-byte buffer", msgLen, len(b))
161			}
162			if msgType == unix.NLMSG_ERROR {
163				if msgLen < unix.SizeofNlMsghdr+4 {
164					return fmt.Errorf("truncated NLMSG_ERROR payload (%d bytes)", msgLen)
165				}
166				code := int32(en.Uint32(b[unix.SizeofNlMsghdr : unix.SizeofNlMsghdr+4]))
167				if code == 0 {
168					return nil
169				}
170				return unix.Errno(-code)
171			}
172			b = b[nlaAlign(msgLen):]
173		}
174		return nil
175	}
176	
177	// installRoutes installs each prefix as a scope-link device route via ifname. It is
178	// called only with a non-empty set (the caller skips a config with no default-route
179	// peer), so a plain tunnel never opens a netlink socket. A failure aborts bring-up
180	// (fail fast) with every already-installed prefix left for Close to withdraw.
181	func installRoutes(ifname string, prefixes []netip.Prefix) error {
182		idx, err := ifIndex(ifname)
183		if err != nil {
184			return err
185		}
186		fd, err := routeSocket()
187		if err != nil {
188			return err
189		}
190		defer func() { _ = unix.Close(fd) }()
191	
192		for i, p := range prefixes {
193			if err := programRoute(fd, uint32(i+1), true, idx, p); err != nil {
194				return fmt.Errorf("install route %s dev %s: %w", p, ifname, err)
195			}
196		}
197		return nil
198	}
199	
200	// removeRoutes withdraws each prefix installed by installRoutes. It is idempotent:
201	// an interface already gone (unix.ENODEV — the non-persistent Close destroyed
202	// wanbond0, taking its routes with it) is a no-op, and a route already dropped
203	// (unix.ESRCH/ENOENT — a double Close, or teardown-with-device) is tolerated per
204	// prefix. Any other error is aggregated and returned so Close can log it.
205	func removeRoutes(ifname string, prefixes []netip.Prefix) error {
206		idx, err := ifIndex(ifname)
207		if err != nil {
208			if errors.Is(err, unix.ENODEV) {
209				return nil
210			}
211			return err
212		}
213		fd, err := routeSocket()
214		if err != nil {
215			return err
216		}
217		defer func() { _ = unix.Close(fd) }()
218	
219		var errs []error
220		for i, p := range prefixes {
221			err := programRoute(fd, uint32(i+1), false, idx, p)
222			if err != nil && !errors.Is(err, unix.ESRCH) && !errors.Is(err, unix.ENOENT) {
223				errs = append(errs, fmt.Errorf("remove route %s dev %s: %w", p, ifname, err))
224			}
225		}
226		return errors.Join(errs...)
227	}
228

1	//go:build !linux
2	
3	package device
4	
5	import (
6		"errors"
7		"net/netip"
8	)
9	
10	// installRoutes/removeRoutes are unavailable off Linux (route programming is done via
11	// rtnetlink, a Linux facility). wanbond targets Linux; these stubs only keep the
12	// package cross-compilable, mirroring linkup_other.go. They are reached only when a
13	// peer opts into mode=default-route (the caller skips them for an empty route set), so
14	// a default (no default-route) config cross-compiles and runs unchanged off Linux.
15	func installRoutes(string, []netip.Prefix) error {
16		return errors.New("device: installing default-route wiring is only supported on Linux")
17	}
18	
19	func removeRoutes(string, []netip.Prefix) error {
20		return errors.New("device: removing default-route wiring is only supported on Linux")
21	}
22

lines: return fd, nil
+// routeMsgFlags returns the netlink header flags for a route request. A delete
+// (add=false) carries only NLM_F_REQUEST|NLM_F_ACK. An add carries additionally
+// NLM_F_CREATE|NLM_F_REPLACE — deliberately NOT NLM_F_EXCL. Under tun_persist=true
+// (T109) an unclean daemon death (SIGKILL/OOM/panic) leaves wanbond0 AND its /1
+// routes in place because Close never ran; on restart an EXCL add would fail EEXIST
+// on the first prefix, abort up(), and wedge every subsequent restart until an
+// operator manually `ip route del`s. REPLACE adopts the leftover route — overwriting
+// any stale attributes so it ends in exactly the intended state — matching the
+// daemon's persistent-TUN adoption posture (persist_linux.go). It also makes a
+// duplicate prefix in the computed set a no-op instead of a hard EEXIST failure.
+// Kept as a pure function so the flag choice is unit-testable without a privileged
+// netlink socket.
+func routeMsgFlags(add bool) uint16 {
+  flags := uint16(unix.NLM_F_REQUEST | unix.NLM_F_ACK)
+  if add {
+    flags |= unix.NLM_F_CREATE | unix.NLM_F_REPLACE
+  return flags
// programRoute sends a single RTM_NEWROUTE (add) or RTM_DELROUTE (remove) request
// for the scope-link device route prefix p via ifindex, then waits for the kernel
// ACK. The request carries exactly two attributes — RTA_DST (the masked prefix
// already-absent route).
func programRoute(fd int, seq uint32, add bool, ifindex int, p netip.Prefix) error {
msgType := uint16(unix.RTM_DELROUTE)
-  flags := uint16(unix.NLM_F_REQUEST | unix.NLM_F_ACK)
if add {
msgType = unix.RTM_NEWROUTE
-    flags |= unix.NLM_F_CREATE | unix.NLM_F_EXCL
+  flags := routeMsgFlags(add)
addr := p.Addr()
family := byte(unix.AF_INET)

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF' && git rev-parse HEAD
fix(device): make default-route install idempotent + clean up partial install (T108 R2)

Address the two lifecycle defects the review panel raised on the T108
default-route wiring.

Criticism 1 — route install was not idempotent. programRoute added with
NLM_F_CREATE|NLM_F_EXCL, so under tun_persist=true an unclean daemon death
left wanbond0 and its two /1 routes in place; on restart the first
RTM_NEWROUTE returned EEXIST, up() aborted, and every subsequent restart
failed identically until an operator ran `ip route del`. Factor the flag
choice into a pure routeMsgFlags() helper that uses NLM_F_CREATE|NLM_F_REPLACE
(never NLM_F_EXCL): re-install now adopts the leftover route and overwrites
any stale attributes, matching the daemon's persistent-TUN adoption posture.
This also makes a duplicate prefix in the computed set a no-op. New
route_linux_test.go asserts the flag choice (privileged netlink socket is
unavailable in the sandbox).

Criticism 2 — partial-install leak + wrong comment. On an install failure
up() returns before the Tunnel is constructed, so Close/removeRoutes never
runs; under tun_persist=true the already-installed prefixes leaked on the
surviving interface. Add a best-effort removeRoutes(name, prefixes) (errors
ignored) on the install-error path in up() before returning, and correct the
now-false installRoutes comment that claimed Close would withdraw the partial
set.

STRICT Q41: routes only — no policy routing/SNAT/forwarding; no config
validation change (multiple-default-route-peers gap stays D59).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

content.text:
Both lifecycle defects are fixed and the gate is green.

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented T108 round-2 fixes in worktree `t108-fix` (reset to round-1 `d825b23`, branched, fixed both reviewer criticisms).
- **Achieved:** pass; resultCommit `9754b0273a4831964108afc14ad4d3d2cfa8efa5`; full gate green — `go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, `go test ./...` (all packages ok), `go build -tags e2e ./...`, `go vet -tags e2e ./...` (exit 0). New `TestRouteMsgFlags` and existing `TestDefaultRoutePrefixes` both pass.
- **Discovered:** The idempotency fix lives entirely in a pure `routeMsgFlags(add bool) uint16` helper (NLM_F_CREATE|NLM_F_REPLACE, never NLM_F_EXCL), which is unit-testable without a privileged netlink socket — the sandbox has no rtnetlink privilege.
